title: THIS is the Best Way to Watch Sports at Cosm Los Angeles and We Toured the Technology Behind it
gist: Cosm operates immersive 27-meter LED dome venues that render Cosm's own high-resolution camera feeds of live sports in real time; Dell supplies the compute behind the experience, and the company aims to scale from two US venues to over 100 worldwide.

themes.theme: Cosm's proprietary capture-and-render model vs. broadcast rebroadcast

insights.claim: Cosm does not rebroadcast an existing network camera feed enlarged; it deploys its own high-resolution cameras and equipment to venues and syncs those feeds to produce an immersive dome experience.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: The vertical integration of capture-to-render is the defensible moat and the main cost driver; it means Cosm needs its own crews and rights at each event, not just a display license.
insights.quote: Cosm does not simply take a camera feed from a major network and display it in a larger format. Instead, Cosm sends its own high-resolution cameras and equipment to venues and then syncs those feeds to give you an immersive experience.
insights.locator: We recently visited Cosm Los Angeles

insights.claim: The dome viewing lets a director reassign the viewer's vantage point across multiple camera angles to follow the action, which the author judged better for watching a game than holding a physical ticket in a fixed seat.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: If dome viewing genuinely beats a mid-tier stadium seat, Cosm competes for live-event spend against ticketing and sports bars, not just cinemas.
insights.quote: in terms of watching a game, this was better than having a ticket because your seat moves with the action.
insights.locator: Basketball coverage offers another perspective advantage

insights.claim: The venue relies on real-time rendering, distributed computing, and high-resolution video workflows, with Dell supplying the compute that powers the experience.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Each new Cosm venue is a recurring workstation/server hardware sale for Dell; a 100-venue rollout is a repeatable enterprise-compute pipeline to watch.
insights.quote: Dell powers the experience, and they made the introduction.

insights.claim: Cosm's core technology and IP derives from planetarium projection systems, which the company has evolved into a physical shared-reality dome for live events.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Roots in planetarium tech signal mature, proven projection IP rather than unproven R&D, lowering technical execution risk on the scale-out.
insights.quote: the company’s technology and IP actually comes from working on planetariums that many of us went to as kids.
insights.locator: In the video, we toured the facility with Devin Poolman

insights.claim: A flexible backend reconfigures content on demand so a single venue can host multiple completely different events, spanning hockey, UFC, soccer, basketball, movie screenings, and real-time 3D renders like the Sistine Chapel, in one day.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: High content flexibility drives venue utilization and revenue per day, the key unit economic for justifying the capex of each dome.
insights.quote: A single day can feature multiple completely different events thanks to a flexible backend infrastructure that reconfigures content on demand.
insights.locator: Visitors entering Cosm encounter a space

insights.claim: Cosm opened its Los Angeles location last year alongside a sister venue in Dallas and states an ambition to build over 100 venues worldwide.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A jump from two venues to 100+ implies a large capex ramp and supplier demand (Dell, LED, cameras); the pace of new openings is the metric that validates or discounts the ambition.
insights.quote: Company leadership aims to bring over 100 Cosm venues worldwide, giving fans everywhere access to this style of experiential viewing.
